coredata.dc:title: Controllable and eco-friendly synthesis of P-riched carbon quantum dots and its application for copper (II) ion sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Fan
He, Xing
Wang, Chunxia
Cao, Yan
Li, Yun
Yan, Linan
Liu, Mengmeng
Lv, Mingzhu
Yang, Yanan
Zhao, Xu
Li, Yongfeng
coredata.dc:description: Eco-friendly phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs) have been successfully synthesized by facial hydrothermal treatment of phytic acid (PA) and sodium citrate. PA is selected because of its hexatomic ring structure with firm C-P bonds, abundant phosphate group and completely natural. In this case, the phosphorus content of the as synthesized P-CQDs achieves 8.52 wt.%. The P-CQDs show a narrow size distribution in the range of 2.5–4.5 nm and exhibit excellent sensitivity to Cu2+ with an ultra-low detection limit of 1 nM. Furthermore, the P-CQDs have been applied in water sample to detect Cu2+, and the results are highly consistent with the data measured by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry. Therefore, the as synthesized P-CQDs display a great potential for applications in the area of Cu2+ ion detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots
Cu2+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of the fluorescence of P-CQDs
Salt tolerance and stability of P-CQDs
Cu2+ sensing by P-CQDs in water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully thank for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21776302 , 21776308 , 21576289 ), the Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum, Beijing (No. 2462017BJB04 , 2462015YQ0306 , 2462016YJRC027 , and C201603 ), Thousand Talents Program and National High-tech R&D Program of China (863 Program, No. 2015AA034603 ), graphical support by Guangchao Li (Wuhan Institute of Physics and Mathematics, Chinese Academy of Sciences). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-vis absorption and (b) photoluminescence emission spectra of P-CQDs.
(a, b) TEM images and (c) a HRTEM image of P-CQDs.
(a) An AFM image of P-CQDs and (b) their height distribution, (c) an AFM image of enlarged P-CQDs and (d) height profile along the dashed line in (c).
XPS survey spectrum (a), C 1s XPS spectrum (b), O 1s XPS spectrum (c), and P 2p XPS spectrum (d) of the as-prepared P-CQDs.
(a, b) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensities of P-CQDs (c, d) Effect of ionic strengths on the fluorescence intensities of P-CQDs.
F/F0 values of the P-CQDs in the absence (black column) and presence (red column) of different metal ions (the concentration of metal ions were 100 μM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of P-CQDs (10 μg/mL) with different concentrations of Cu2+ (from top to bottom: 0, 0.002, 0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.2, 10, 20, 80, 100, 320, 500, 750, 1000, 1500, 2000, 10000 μM), (b) the plot for F0/F and F/F0 versus different concentrations of Cu2+.
(a) Preparation route and structure sketch of P-CQDs, (b) Mechanism sketch of P-CQDs quenches by Cu2+ and Cu2+ chelates by EDTA.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Controllable and eco-friendly synthesis of P-riched carbon quantum dots and its application for copper (II) ion sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum, Beijing, Changping 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Drinking Water Sciences and Technology, Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100085, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Drinking Water Sciences and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Eco-friendly phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs) have been successfully synthesized by facial hydrothermal treatment of phytic acid (PA) and sodium citrate. PA is selected because of its hexatomic ring structure with firm C-P bonds, abundant phosphate group and completely natural. In this case, the phosphorus content of the as synthesized P-CQDs achieves 8.52 wt.%. The P-CQDs show a narrow size distribution in the range of 2.5–4.5 nm and exhibit excellent sensitivity to Cu2+ with an ultra-low detection limit of 1 nM. Furthermore, the P-CQDs have been applied in water sample to detect Cu2+, and the results are highly consistent with the data measured by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry. Therefore, the as synthesized P-CQDs display a great potential for applications in the area of Cu2+ ion detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a fascinating carbon-based nanomaterial with diameters less than 10 nm, were first obtained from single-walled carbon nanotubes by an arc-discharge method in 2004 [1]. In recent years, CQDs have received tremendous attention due to their superiorities such as low toxicity, high chemical inertness, well water solubility, good photostability, unique photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, cell membrane permeability and tunable surface functionalities relative to traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots. Consequently, CQDs have been widely used in fluorescent probing, bioimaging [2], drug delivery [3], biosensing [4–8], antibacterial [9], batteries [10], thermal theranostics [11], photocatalysis [12] and electrocatalysis [13]. In order to develop an efficient way for synthesis of carbon quantum dots, various synthetic methods, such as hydrothermal [14,15], microwave [16], electrochemical synthesis [17,18], supported synthesis [19], laser ablation [20] and chemical oxidation [21] have been explored. However, obstacles are notably, including the toxic precursors, uncontrollable particle size, low production yield and shifting emission.

Nowadays, great efforts, such as surface passivation, functionalization and heteroatom doping, have been paid to modify the CQDs for enhancing their properties. Among these approaches, many evidences have confirmed that heteroatom doped into the CQDs can change their electronic structures [22], which serve as an effective way to tune the properties of CQDs. Additionally, the heteroatom doping has been supposed to be the most promising way for modification of CQDs owing to its facile nature as well as low cost, which has gained considerable attention. In the past studies, heteroatom doping into CQDs such as nitrogen (N) [23], sulfur (S) [24], boron (B) [25] and phosphorus (P) [26], have been realized. Compared with the other heteroatoms, phosphorus atom is larger, which can form substitutional defects in diamond sp3 thin films, behaving as an n-type donor and thereby modifying the electronic and optical properties. Moreover, in view of the remarkable quantum-confinement and edge effects of carbon quantum dots, doping CQDs with P atoms can alter their electronic characteristics and offer more active sites, thus yielding new and unexpected properties [26]. Up to now, several kinds of P-doped CQDs were produced using phosphorus-containing precursors, such as phosphorous tribromide [26], monosodium phosphate [27] and phosphoric acid [28,29]. However, these suffer from several limitations, such as low doping content, complex synthesis process, less sensibility and low photostability, which impose restrictions on their practical applications. It is known that phosphorus can be fixed and doped more effectively when use triphenylphosphine as precursors due to the strong P-C bonds [30,31], being used for synthesis P-CQDs [32]. However, the triphenylphosphine is toxic and has a high cost. Therefore, the development of excellent phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs) fluorescence sensing materials is highly desirable to overcome the above limitations.

PA, an ideal phosphorus source with firm C-P bonds and abundant P content, is a completely natural and edible grade material that can be easily dissolved in water, which has been used in synthesized phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (PN-CQDs) [16]. Herein, we use PA and sodium citrate to synthesis a P-riched carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal method. The whole process is simple, low cost and eco-friendly. At last, the content of P in the as synthesized P-CQDs reaches as high as 8.52 wt.%. What’s more, The P-CQDs have a narrow size distribution of 2.5–4.5 nm, which emit strong blue luminescence at 429 nm. Then, we have further confirmed that such P-CQDs can serve as a very effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ ions with detection limit as low as 1 nM. This fluorescent sensing method can be also successfully used to detect Cu2+ ions in real water samples.

Phytic acid (99.5%) and sodium citrate (99.5%) were purchased from Aladdin. Quinine sulfate was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and metal salts used in this work include CuCl2·2H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, ZnCl2, AgNO3, KCl, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, AlCl3, BaCl2·2H2O, NaCl, SnCl4·5H2O, Mg(NO3)2, FeCl3·2H2O and CoCl2·2H2O were purchased from Aladdin. All other reagents were analytical grade and were used without further purification.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were carried out on an X-ray diffraction fluorescence spectroscope (Hitachi, F-7000). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2700). The FT-IR spectra of the P-CQDs measured by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker, Tensor II). The morphologies of the synthesized P-CQDs were characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, F20) and atomic force microscopes (AFM, collected with a Bruker Multimode 8). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher K-Alpha American with an Al K X-ray source). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, NETZSCH STA409PC) was performed under Ar atmosphere with the increasing temperature rate at 10 °C per min. The concentration of Cu2+ ions in water sample and the content of phosphorus in P-CQDs were analyzed by an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, Optima 7300 V).

As shown in Scheme 1 a, P-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of PA and sodium citrate which were chosen as the phosphorus and carbon source. During the process, the formation of P-CQDs was likely through four steps, including dehydration, polymerization, carbonization and surface passivation [33–35]. In a typical procedure, 4 mmol sodium citrate and x mmol (x = 0.5, 1, 2, 4) phytic acid (PA) were dissolved into 25 ml deionized (DI) water and stirred to form a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution was transferred into 50 ml Teflon lined stainless autoclave. Subsequently, the sealed autoclave was heated to a fixed temperature (T = 160, 200 and 240 °C) in an electric oven and kept for an additional 4 h. The obtained P-CQDs were denoted as P-CQDs (T, 4/x), and the P-CQDs (200 °C, 4/1), P-CQDs (160 °C, 4/1), P-CQDs (240 °C, 4/1), P-CQDs (200 °C, 4/0.5), P-CQDs (200 °C, 4/2), P-CQDs (200 °C, 4/4), were denoted as P-CQDs-1, P-CQDs-2, P-CQDs-3, P-CQDs-4, P-CQDs-5 and P-CQDs-6, respectively. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, then filtered through 0.22 μm membrane via millipore syringe filters and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO 3500 Da) for 48 h to remove the remaining salts and tiny fragments to obtain a P-CQDs solution. After filtration and dialysis, the P-CQDs show a transparent light brown solution and emit blue fluorescence under UV lamp.

Determination of fluorescence quantum yield (QY) measurements were performed according to the previously established procedure. Quinine sulfate dispersed in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 54%) was used as a standard as reported [34]. The absorbance of the solutions for P-CQDs and quinine sulfate was kept below 0.1 at 360 nm.

Typically, a fixed concentration of P-CQDs (200 μL, 50 μg/mL) was added into 2 ml aqueous buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.0) at room temperature, and then different concentrations of Cu2+ (200 μL) were added. The resulting solution was shaken for several minutes to ensure completely homogeneous. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was stand still for 10 min prior to measurement. For comparison, the same procedure was also performed for blank experiments. All experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the standard deviation was plotted as the error bar.

To evaluate the feasibility of the proposed method for analysis of Cu2+ in a real system, the P-CQDs based nanosensors were applied to detect Cu2+ in water sample. The water sample was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane (Millipore syringe filters) prior to the detection, then different concentrations of Cu2+ was added to the above water sample. The spiked samples were then diluted to 1 mL with water containing P-CQDs (5 μg/mL) and then analyzed by using the ICP-OES. All the fluorescence measurements were carried out at room temperature.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the P-CQDs exhibits two peaks at 238 nm and 330 nm. The sharp peak at 238 nm is attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the CC bond [22], and the broad one at 330 nm is attributed to the trapping of excited state energy at the surface states, leading to a strong blue emission [24,36]. We find that P-CQDs dispersion appears colorless under day light and shows strong emission under UV lamp (Fig. 1a, inset), suggesting that the P-CQDs exhibits a well dispersity in aqueous solution and can be applied to homogeneous analysis. To further explore the optical properties of water soluble P-CQDs, a detailed PL investigation was carried out with different excitation wavelength. The photoluminescence (PL) of the P-CQDs is excited at wavelengths ranging from 300 to 450 nm, as shown in Fig. 1b, in which the maximum emission intensity increases remarkably with the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 330 nm, while the fluorescence intensity decreases with the increase in the excitation wavelength from 330 to 450 nm accompanying with the red-shift of the emission peak, this phenomenon is similar to that of the reported excitation wavelength dependent PL of other un-doped carbon dos [34,36]. The following experiment will utilize 330 nm as the excitation wavelength as it emits the maximum intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the fluorescence of P-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to find suitable synthesis conditions for P-CQDs, the hydrothermal process at different temperatures (T = 160, 200 and 240 °C) and different molar ratios of precursors (sodium citrate /PA = 4/x, x = 0.5, 1, 2, 4) have been investigated. Among these samples, the strongest PL intensity of synthesized P-CQDs has been obtained when the hydrothermal process was performed with the 4 mmol sodium citrate and 1 mmol PA in 25 mL DI water at 200 °C for 4 h (P-CQDs-1) (Figs. S1, S2, S3 and Table S3). As shown in Fig. S1, the P-CQDs-2 prepared at 160 °C shows weak PL intensity maybe due to the unshaped nanoparticles, and the weak PL intensity of the P-CQDs-3 prepared at 240 °C maybe result from the carbon dots agglomerated in high temperature. Moreover, the PL intensity of P-CQDs-4, P-CQDs-5 and P-CQDs-6 were weaker than P-CQDs-1 (Fig. S2), being ascribed to the trapping of excited state energy at the surface states [24,36]. The brightness of the PL was quantified in terms of the emission quantum yield (Fig. S3 and Table S3). The QY was determined by slope method by the reference of quinine sulfate [37]: compared the integrated photoluminescence intensity (360 nm excitation) and the absorbance value [several values (less than 0.1 at excitation wavelength) gave the curve] of the CDs samples with that of the references. Then used the Eq. (1):
(1)
Φ
x
=
Φ
st
(
K
x
/
K
st
)
(
η
x
/
η
st
)
2
where Φ is the QY, K is the slope determined by the curves and η is the refractive index. The subscript “st” refers to quinine sulfate and “x” refers to the samples. For these aqueous solutions, ηx/ηst = 1. So the equation was simplified to (2):
(2)
Φ
x
=
Φ
st
(
K
x
/
K
st
)

As shown in Fig. S3 and Table S3. The quantum yield of P-CQDs-1 is estimated to be 3.5% at 360 nm excitation by using quinine sulfate (54%) as a standard reference (for simplify, all the P-CQDs refers later are stand for P-CQDs-1). The result is higher than that of contrast samples (P-CQDs-2 to P-CQDs-6) and comparable than those of fluorescent carbon dots reported previously [36,38], which is benefit for sensitively ion detection.

As shown in Fig. 2 a and 2b, the P-CQDs are spherical and well monodispersed. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 2c shows that the prepared P-CQDs displayed a highly crystalline structure, with a 0.234 nm lattice parameter, which is similar to the diffraction plane of graphitic (sp2) carbon (102) [37]. The TEM results are further confirmed by AFM investigation. As shown in Fig. 3 , the selected area of AFM indicates that the thickness of P-CQDs is distributed mainly in a narrow range of 2.5–4.5 nm, and almost 30% of the P-CQDs are centered at an average size of 3.8 nm. It is noted that the energy gap gradually increases with the decrease of particle size, and the smaller particle size can cause a blue-shift of fluorescence, leading to a strong blue fluorescence emission [38–40]. Therefore, the as-synthesized P-CQDs with small size and narrow distribution can emit strong blue fluorescence, in agreement with our optical results. Due to these excellent fluorescent properties, the P-CQDs are very prospective to be applied in the ions detection and bioimaging fields.

In the FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 4 , the broad band at 3250–3750 cm−1 can be assigned to stretching vibrations of O-H, indicating that there are a lot of hydroxyl groups on the surface of P-CQDs. It can be widely accepted that the peaks at about 1615, 1384 and 1254 cm−1 correspond to CO/CC stretching, CO stretching in carboxylic acid or carboxylate and COH/COC stretching, respectively [37]. Meanwhile, the typical stretching of CH is assigned to the absorption band at 2922 cm−1. Moreover, the peaks at 1121 cm−1, 1056 cm−1 and 804 cm−1 are related to the vibration of PO, PO, POH of phosphate containing functional groups, respectively. Simultaneously, the stretching vibration of P-C results in the peak at 613 cm−1, indicating that phosphate is successfully doped in the P-CQDs structure. The FT-IR results indicate that the P-CQDs are rich in hydroxyl, carbonyl, and phosphoric acid groups on their surfaces, which can improve the P-CQDs hydrophilicity and stability in an aqueous system.

The XPS survey spectrum of P-CQDs in Fig. 5 a shows four characteristic peaks at about 133, 191, 284.9 and 531 eV, which correspond to the P 2p, P 2s, C 1s and O 1s, respectively. The concentration of phosphorus in P-CQDs is 5.16 at.% (10.87 wt.%) and oxygen is 43.18 at.% (46.97 wt.%) (Table S1), the content of phosphorus is also in accordance with the result obtained by ICP-OES (8.52 wt.%) (Table S2), which are higher than the pervious reports [26–30]. As shown in Fig. 5b, deconvolution of the P 2p peak can be divided into two peaks at 133.1 eV and 134.8 eV, which can be assigned to P-C and P-O groups [41–43]. The existence of P-C peak suggests that the phosphorus atoms are incorporated into the graphene framework via a hydrothermal process. As revealed in Fig. 5c, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum can be well fitted with sp2 CC (from aromatic structure), and the CC/CP, CO, CO and OCO structures are centered at 284.6, 285.6, 286.1, 287.9 and 288.5 eV, respectively [37]. The CO bond may correspond to epoxy and tertiary alcohol functional groups on the basal plane, as well as phenol in the periphery. The CO and OCO bonds indicate the presence of ketone and carboxylic groups in the graphene periphery. The composition of different carbon bonds (Table S1) reflected the fact that most of the oxygen functional groups were located on the edge sites of the P-CQDs. The O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5d) can be deconvoluted into four peaks, a CO/PO at 530.9 eV, a CO at 532 eV, a C-OH/P-OH at 533.1 eV and a COOH/Water at 535.3 eV, respectively [44]. These results illustrate that CP, PO and CO are indeed covalently bonds to the P-CQDs. Combined with XPS and FT-IR spectra, it is deduced that the electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity of P-CQDs might be improved due to the introduction of P atoms and the abundant surface oxygen containing groups.

In addition, the TGA curve has been measured to determine the stability of P-CQDs under Ar gas. As shown in Fig. 6 , the slight mass loss below 150 °C can be attributed to the evaporation of the adsorbed water. The sharp mass loss between 150 °C and 200 °C is proposed to be the presence of crystal water in the P-CQDs. The significant mass loss between 200 °C and 530 °C can be assigned to some complex chemical reactions, including thermal degradation of P-CQDs which includes the chain scission, hydrolysis/alcoholysis of carbonate linkage, reformation of carbonate linkage, and branching and crosslinking reaction of the molecular chains of P-CQDs [45]. Then, the crosslinking polyaromatic carbonaceous residue is formed via crosslinking reactions at the higher temperature. Thus, the mass of P-CQDs stays constant after 600 °C, indicating that the rest of them are stable carbon structures.

In order to figure out the optimized conditions for ions detections, various factors possibly affect the fluorescence intensity were investigated. As the different pH may play a critical role in the fluorescence intensity, various pH values were introduced to monitor the fluorescence variation under 330 nm excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the fluorescence intensities of P-CQDs changes with the pH vary from 2 to 11. The fluorescence intensity increases with the increase in the pH value. Compared with the fluorescence intensity at pH 11, the fluorescence intensity shows 63.2% decrease at pH 2, revealing that the as synthesized P-CQDs exhibit higher sensibility and better linearity than those reported in the previous literatures [25,46]. Moreover, the maximum wavelength of P-CQDs shows a blue shift when the pH value increases from 2 to 11. The pH-responsive properties of P-CQDs can be explained by the change in the surface charge of P-CQDs caused by protonation-deprotonation [47–50]. The functional groups such as carboxyl and phosphoric anhydride on the surface of P-CQDs can result in protonation and deprotonation as the pH of P-CQDs solutions change. The changing state of groups on the surface of CQDs can result in these different photoluminescence behaviors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Salt tolerance and stability of P-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7c and d show that the P-CQDs keep stable even at a KCl concentration of 2 M. The stability test of the obtained P-CQDs has been carried out by exposing the P-CQDs aqueous solution to UV irradiation (365 nm, 16 W). As shown in Fig. 7e and f, no obvious decrease in the fluorescence intensity of P-CQDs is observed upon UV irradiation for 90 min, and only 1.2% decrease in the fluorescence intensity is observed upon exposure to UV irradiation for 120 min. These results suggested that the P-CQDs exhibited excellent stability against iron strengths and UV irradiation.

The selectivity of P-CQDS in buffer solution for various metal ions was investigated by fluorescence measurements. Different metal ions (Cu2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ag+, K+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Na+, Sn4+, Mg2+, Fe3+ and Co2+ (50 μM)) have been prepared to investigate the ion sensing ability of the as-prepared P-CQDs. As displayed in Fig. 8 , the fluorescence intensity was nearly unchanged in the presence of other metal ions, whereas the addition of Cu2+ results in a considerable fluorescence quenching of P-CQDs than that of the other metal ions, implying that the as-prepared P-CQDs show high selectivity towards Cu2+ over other metal ions. It is well known that the detection of Cu2+ is crucial due to the high toxicity of Cu2+ to organism, such as certain algae, fungi, and many bacteria and viruses [51–55]. Conventional metal-based quantum dots (QDs) including CdS, CdSe, ZnS and CdSeTe, have been successfully used as optical sensing nanomaterials [56–59]. However, they suffer from several drawbacks, such as involvement of extra process for particle surface modification and thus time-consuming. The toxicity of heavy metal elements can bring environmental and health risks. Accordingly, the development of new nontoxic optical sensing P-CQDs for Cu2+ detection is highly desirable.

The strong blue emission of P-CQDs can be quenched obviously when Cu2+ are added into the solution (Scheme 1b). The fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Cu2+ may result from the higher binding affinity and faster chelating kinetics with P/O functional groups on the P-CQDs surface, which facilitate aggregation to form P-CQDs-Cu2+ chelate [60–63]. After that, the electrons in the excited state of the P-CQDs are transferred to the unfilled 3d orbitals of Cu2+, thus facilitating the non-radiative electron-hole recombination and causing significant fluorescence quenching, as follows in Eq. (3):
(3)
4
CQD-OH +
Cu
2 +
→
[ Cu
( OCQD )
4
]
2 -
+ 4
H
+

Meanwhile, as indicated in Fig. 9 a, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared P-CQDs at 330 nm is gradually quenched with the increase of Cu2+ ion concentration (0–10 mM). To evaluate the sensitivity of the as-prepared fluorescent P-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity spectra of the obtained P-CQDs upon addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ are recorded using fluorescence titrations after 10 min of incubation. As shown in Fig. 9b. the fluorescence ratio F0/F is gradually decreased when the concentration of Cu2+ increases from 0–10 mM, in which F0 and F are defined as the fluorescence intensities of the as-prepared P-CQDs at 330 nm in the absence and the presence of metal ions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9b, a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) and Cu2+ ion concentration in the range of 0–20 nM is observed. The corresponding regression coefficient (R2) between different concentrations of Cu2+ ions and the fluorescence intensity is 0.99297. These results indicate that the as-prepared P-CQDs are promising for Cu2+ ions detection in aqueous media. Meanwhile, the quenching efficiency of the Cu2+ ions was defined as the following correlation Eq. (4):
(4)
F /
F
0
= 0.98691 - 0.00954 ×
C
Cu
2 +
where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of the obtained P-CQDs in the presence and absence of Cu2+ ion, respectively. The detection limit (LOD) to Cu2+ is calculated by employing the following Eq. (5):
(5)
LOD = 3 σ / K

where σ and K are defined as the standard deviation of (F/F0) values and K represents the slope of the linear line, respectively. The LOD of Cu2+ was estimated to be 1 nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Overall, the results reveal a higher sensitivity to Cu2+ than most of those obtained by other fluorescent probes, such as semiconductor quantum dots (0.005–7.1 μΜ) [53,56–59], carbon dots (0.01–0.71 μΜ) [64–68] and doped carbon dots (0.006–0.3 μΜ) [69–75] (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ sensing by P-CQDs in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the detection performance of P-CQDs for Cu2+ in aqueous buffer solution, we proceeded to investigate the detection potential of P-CQDs in the real samples. Thus, the P-CQDs have been further investigated in water sample in order to generalize the sensing ability of P-CQDs for Cu2+. The P-CQDs was exposed to an aqueous solution of spiked water samples and the corresponding emission changes of P-CQDs were monitored. The spiked water were diluted 50 and 100 times. Similar to the case of the detection in solution, as the spiked water sample added, the fluorescence intensity decreased. The relationship between the quenched fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Cu2+ was determined quantitatively. The P-CQDs based sensing approach provides a linear response to Cu2+ ions in spiked samples at concentrations over the range from 0 to 20 nM (F/F0 = 0.98691–0.00954 × C Cu 2+, R2 = 0.99297). The concentration of Cu2+ ions in the water sample which were diluted 50 and 100 times (each sample was tested n = 10 times) detected by P-CQDs are estimated to be 18.4 ± 0.85 nM (or 1.17 ± 0.05 ppb) and 9.25 ± 0.85 nM (or 0.59 ± 0.05 ppb). Thus the concentration of Cu2+ in real water sample is calculated to be 0.923 ± 0.085 μM (or 58.6 ± 5.40 ppb), and this value is well consistent with the results obtained by the ICP-OES method, 0.954 ± 0.065 μM (or 61.3 ± 4.13 ppb). The result demonstrates that P-CQDs can be used for the detection of Cu2+ in real water samples with high sensitivity and accuracy.

In summary, P-CQDs have been successfully synthesized by facile one-pot hydrothermal treating of phytic acid and sodium citrate for the first time. The as-prepared P-CQDs have a very narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 3.8 nm, and exhibit excellent stable property even under extreme ionic strengths and light illumination. By doping of phosphorus atoms in a content of 8.52 wt.%, the electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity of P-CQDs have been substantially improved, which is indeed beneficial for chemically binding between P-CQDs and Cu2+. The P-CQDs emitting strong blue fluorescence can be used as a novel nanosensor for the highly sensitive detection of Cu2+ with detection limit as low as 1 nM. As a result, the obtained P-CQDs demonstrate much higher sensibility in Cu2+ detection compared to that in previous reports. Moreover, the rich phosphorus doped CQDs with altered electronic characteristics can offer more active sites, are also expected to emerge new properties in catalysis and photovoltaic device.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully thank for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21776302, 21776308, 21576289), the Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum, Beijing (No. 2462017BJB04, 2462015YQ0306, 2462016YJRC027, and C201603), Thousand Talents Program and National High-tech R&D Program of China (863 Program, No. 2015AA034603), graphical support by Guangchao Li (Wuhan Institute of Physics and Mathematics, Chinese Academy of Sciences).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.03.246.
